data.title: Biomea Fusion Reports Positive Phase II Data For Diabetes Drug Icovamenib
data.description:
The Phase II  COVALENT-111 study results highlighted that icovamenib maintained a favorable safety profile throughout the 52-week observation period, with no serious treatment-related adverse events.

Additionally, the study showed statistically significant reductions in HbA1c levels among certain patient subgroups, suggesting icovamenib’s potential to transform diabetes treatment.

“We are encouraged by the durability of icovamenib’s effect observed nine months post-dosing at Week 52,” said Mick Hitchcock, Ph.D., Interim CEO and Board Member of Biomea Fusion. “We believe that we now have in hand initial evidence of durable efficacy, additional favorable safety data, a clear understanding of an effective dose, and most importantly, the target patient populations.”

The presentation at the 19th International Conference on Advanced Technologies & Treatments for Diabetes included data indicating that severe insulin-deficient patients experienced an HbA1c reduction of 1.2% at Week 52, with the most effective dosing regimen achieving a mean reduction of 1.5%.

This positive data is expected to bolster investor confidence as the company prepares for upcoming Phase II studies targeting specific patient populations.

Technical Analysis

As of Friday, the stock traded 2.9% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 4.0% above its 100-day SMA, indicating short-term strength.

Over the past 12 months, shares have decreased by 46.46%, and they are currently positioned closer to their 52-week lows than highs.

The RSI is at 50.74, which is considered neutral territory, suggesting a balanced market sentiment. Meanwhile, MACD is at 0.0342, above its signal line at 0.0313, indicating bullish momentum as the histogram shows a slight positive trend.

The combination of neutral RSI and bullish MACD suggests mixed momentum, indicating potential for upward movement if buying pressure increases.

  • Key Resistance: $1.50
  • Key Support: $1.00

Sector Performance

Biomea Fusion is part of the Healthcare sector, which closed down 0.17% on Friday, ranking 6th out of 11 sectors. The sector has faced challenges recently, with a 30-day performance decline of 3.98%, reflecting broader headwinds affecting healthcare stocks.

Despite the stock’s positive movement, it is underperforming relative to the sector, which indicates that while Biomea Fusion is gaining traction, it is not fully capitalizing on the sector’s potential recovery.

Biomea Fusion is a clinical-stage diabetes and obesity medicines company focused on the discovery and development of oral covalent small-molecule drugs to treat patients with metabolic diseases. The company uses its proprietary FUSION System discovery platform to develop a pipeline of novel small-molecule product candidates.

Its principal clinical program’s drug candidate, icovamenib, is being developed as an orally bioavailable, selective covalent inhibitor of menin in two clinical and multiple preclinical studies, investigating icovamenib’s potential in type 1 and type 2 diabetes, as well as its impact in obesity.

The company is also developing BMF-650, an investigational, oral small-molecule GLP-1 RA, which has the potential to be a therapeutic option for diabetes and obesity.

Earnings & Analyst Outlook

Biomea Fusion is slated to provide its next financial update on March 30, 2026 (estimated).

  • EPS Estimate: Loss of 23 cents (up from Loss of 81 cents)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating. Recent analyst moves include:

  • D. Boral Capital: Buy (Maintains Target to $12.00) (Jan. 13)
  • Citigroup: Buy (Lowers Target to $6.00) (Nov. 10, 2025)
  • D. Boral Capital: Buy (Lowers Target to $12.00) (Nov. 5, 2025)

Price Action

BMEA Stock Price Activity: Biomea Fusion shares were up 3.68% at $1.41 on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: Palmer Luckey Turned His Side Hustle That Began Life As A Teen-Run Online Forum Into A $1 Billion Valuation Opportunity: Here's How He Did It
data.description:
Palmer Luckey is best known for the billion-dollar sale of Oculus and for building defense giant Anduril. But long before those headline-grabbing bets, he had a side hustle, a retro-gaming project he started as a teenager that is now reportedly in talks for funding at a $1 billion valuation.

The Teenage Hobby He Never Dropped

ModRetro did not begin as a polished startup deck. It started as a teen-run online forum for video game console modifications, built around Luckey's obsession with old-school gaming hardware. Years later, even as he built Oculus and then Anduril, he kept returning to that side project instead of letting it die in the usual graveyard of "someday" ideas.

That is the real lesson here is that not every side hustle begins as a startup idea. Sometimes it starts as a nerdy fixation you keep feeding on nights and weekends while your main career does the heavy lifting. Luckey later said he had been working on the "ultimate Game Boy-inspired device" off and on as a hobby for almost 17 years.

How A Passion Project Became Real Money

Now comes the question of scale. The Financial Times reported that ModRetro is discussing a new round that could value it at $1 billion, with a reimagined Nintendo 64-style console expected next.

What Everyday Builders Can Take From It

Luckey's story is not about quitting your job and chasing a dream without working out the basics. It is closer to building the thing you cannot stop caring about, even when your résumé gets louder than your hobby. It’s about the possibility of a side hustle quietly becoming a major sequel in the background.

Photo Courtesy: Pressmaster on Shutterstock.com

data.title: EXCLUSIVE: Just 5 Female CEOs In A 50-Stock ETF — C-Suite Execs Explain The Leadership Gap In Corporate America
data.description:
One week after the International Women’s day, here is a stark reminder how the C-suite remains underrepresented by women. Data from a leadership-focused ETF underscores how uncommon female leadership remains among large U.S. companies.

The ETF tracks companies ranked through a leadership execution framework developed by Indiggo. Janeen Gelbart, co-founder and CEO of the firm, said the methodology evaluates corporate leadership broadly rather than focusing on individual executives.

"The ReturnOnLeadership model evaluates corporate leadership as a whole and is individual leader agnostic. One of the benefits of this is it allows us to maintain complete objectivity in the way we measure and rank corporations," Gelbart told Benzinga.

"Elements such as strategic clarity, leadership alignment and focused action are all conduits to superior leadership execution and optimal outcomes," Gelbart said.

She added that leadership has historically been difficult to quantify for investors.

"Leadership has historically been hard to quantify and measure, yet has an ongoing direct impact on real outcomes. We have been able to break through that barrier with a proven and tested product that consistently outperforms," she said.

Leadership As An Investable Factor

The ETF itself tracks the Solactive Indiggo ReturnOnLeadership U.S. Large-Cap Index and follows a rules-based methodology.

Cullen Rodgers, chief investment officer at Wedbush Fund Advisers and portfolio manager of the ETF, said the strategy evaluates a different dimension than traditional factor investing.

"Traditional factor strategies typically rely on financial ratios or market characteristics such as profitability, balance sheet strength, or valuation. Those approaches measure the outcomes a company produces. The index underlying EXEQ looks at a different layer of concrete signals, organizational execution," Rodgers said.

"Because the methodology is rules based and passive, the portfolio reflects the outcome of the index ranking rather than subjective assessments of individual executives or personalities," Rodgers said.

Rodgers said the approach introduces a structured way to analyze leadership.

"Leadership is not a trend. It is an eternal, highly influential driver of outcomes."

data.title: Think You Need $1M in Your 401(k)? The Average Is Nowhere Near That — How Do You Compare?
data.description:
If your balance hasn’t hit seven figures, take a deep breath. You aren’t failing; you’re part of the actual majority. Recent reports from major providers like Empower, Vanguard, and Fidelity show that the average saver is operating in a much more grounded financial landscape.

Don't Miss:

The Great Disconnect: Average vs. Reality

When looking at retirement data, the “average” can be a bit of a liar. High earners with massive balances often skew the numbers upward, making the middle class feel further behind than they actually are. That is why the median—the true middle point where half of savers are above and half are below—is the number you should actually be watching.

According to Vanguard “How America Saves” 2025 snapshots, the overall average 401(k) balance sits at $167,970. However, the median is a much more realistic $44,115. While strong markets recently boosted these figures, they remain a far cry from the million-dollar dream.

Fidelity's latest analysis of nearly 25 million accounts tells a similar story. Even the most seasoned savers aren’t hitting the million-mark on average:

  • Generation X: Average balances hover around $222,100.
  • Baby Boomers: Average balances sit near $270,800.

Peak Earning Years and the Savings Ceiling

Empower's latest dashboard data, which typically tracks highly engaged savers, shows higher numbers but reinforces the same trend. Even during the peak earning years of the 50s, the average balance is $629,000. While impressive, it is still hundreds of thousands of dollars short of the $1 million goal.

Here is how savers stack up by age group:

  • 20s: The average is $116,872, The median is $43,192
  • 30s: The average is $212,356, the median is $78,857
  • 40s: The average is $409,686, the median is $156,675
  • 50s: The average is $629,000, the median is $246,554
  • 60s: The average is $576,755, the median is $187,249

By the time savers hit their 70s, the median drops to $95,931 as retirees begin drawing down their funds.

Trending: Before you make an offer, ask these 6 questions every homebuyer should know — or face serious regret later.

Why the $1 Million Benchmark Persists

The obsession with the million-dollar finish line usually stems from the guidelines that suggest you should have 10 times your final salary saved by age 67. If you earn $100,000 a year, the math leads you straight to $1 million.

However, several factors keep the national average lower:

  • Inconsistent Contributions: Life happens. Job changes, market volatility, and emergency hardship withdrawals can stall momentum.
  • Late Starts: Many workers do not find their “savings stride” until their 30s or 40s.
  • The Median Gap: Because the “average” is skewed by the top 1%, the typical worker is actually doing quite well if they are anywhere near the median for their age group.

The Modern Silver Lining

It isn’t all uphill. Recent IRS updates are making it easier to play catch-up. Contribution limits have climbed to $24,500, with an additional $11,250 catch-up deferral available for those aged 60 to 63 under updated federal rules.

Despite economic headwinds, Americans are staying disciplined, keeping contribution rates steady at 8% to 10% of their pay when including employer matches, according to Fidelity. 

The Finish Line Looks Different For Everyone

If you're staring at your 401(k) and feeling like you're losing a race, remember that the “finish line” is personalized. Retirement isn’t about hitting a specific number broadcast on the news; it's about covering your specific lifestyle, managing your home equity, and utilizing other assets like IRAs or pensions.

The data is clear. Most of your neighbors do not have $1 million in their 401(k). If you are at or above the median for your age group, you aren’t just “okay”—you're ahead of the curve.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: A Man Complains He Pays For Every Date With His Girlfriend, Even 'In This Economy.' Dave Ramsey Responds, 'Is She A Money-Hungry Gold Digger?'
data.description:
Paying for dinner on a date is one thing. Paying for everything in a relationship is another.

Oscar from Canada says that’s exactly the situation he found himself in after five months of dating his girlfriend. On a recent episode of “The Ramsey Show,” he said he has covered nearly every expense, and it’s starting to bother him, especially given today’s economic pressures.

Cultural Expectations Or A Problem?

Oscar, who is originally from Iran, explained that his girlfriend, who has Polish roots but grew up in Canada, told him early on that in her culture men typically pay for dates.

While he said he has some traditional views himself, the arrangement started to feel unbalanced.

Don't Miss:

“Since day one, I’ve been paying for restaurants, going out, activities, snacks and everything,” he said. “I’m traditional sort of, but at the same time, I don’t see the point of man paying for everything, especially in this economy.”

The concern grew when the couple began discussing a potential trip to Europe. According to Oscar, his girlfriend suggested he pay for the flights and hotel while she would cover food during the trip.

Given that flights and hotels make up most of the cost of a trip, Oscar said it would result in him covering about 85% to 90% of the total expense.

Personal finance expert Dave Ramsey first approached the situation from a broader relationship perspective. In his view, the strongest relationships aren’t about tracking who pays for what.

“The marriages that I know, that I consider some of the best marriages on the planet, are where each of the people involved make the relationship about how much they can serve the other person,” Ramsey said. “How good can I be to you? Instead of what am I getting out of this?”

Trending: Think you're saving enough for your kids? You might be dangerously off — see why

The Real Red Flag

Ramsey acknowledged that cultural expectations can play a role in dating habits. He pointed out that in the Southern U.S., where he grew up, it was common for men to pay for everything.

“I’m an old southern redneck,” Ramsey said. “In our world, 100% the guy pays for everything. Period. That’s called chivalry in our world.”

But he also said there is a clear line between generosity and entitlement.

“The last thing I want is a high-maintenance princess that’s entitled,” Ramsey said. “That’s cray cray, I’m going to avoid that woman like the plague.”

At one point, Ramsey asked Oscar directly: “Is she a money-hungry gold digger?”

Oscar answered that he believed she was “money hungry.” Even so, Ramsey later said he didn’t necessarily hear clear signs of a gold digger based on the overall description. Instead, he suggested the larger issue might be whether the couple shares the same values around money.

“If you can’t get aligned on the handling of money and the value of money in this relationship, that is a red flag for any relationship,” Ramsey said.

Conversations about money can be uncomfortable, especially early in a relationship. Platforms like Money Pickle connect users with fiduciary financial advisors who can help couples talk through budgeting, financial goals and spending habits so they can build a plan that works for both partners.

Co-host George Kamel suggested a practical way to test whether spending expectations were becoming a problem. He recommended choosing simpler, low-cost dates and seeing how the girlfriend responds.

“Set up the boundaries earlier on,” Kamel said. “Say, ‘Hey, you want to just take a walk in the park?'”

In the end, Ramsey said the biggest concern isn’t who pays for dinner. The real issue is whether both people see money the same way.

Image: Shutterstock
